item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything about this is fantastic. The concerningly brief ConProcs, the realistic dialogue, and the subtle but dread-inducing supplements blew me away.

Again, fantastic job, and a giddy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really shouldn't have ignored the supplemental documentation on my first read. I know you wouldn't write them for nothing, but they didn't seem important at first and the info was always restricted…

A wonderful interweaving of two tales, with twists, mistakes, and despair on both sides. From what I can tell, an emergency happened above the tunnel that required power to be shut off, which the D-class refused to do.

Possible typos
– hide block

Koullette: I didn't expect you [to] remember the diagram labels.

D-3481: I just woke up [and] the entire tunnel is filled with bats.

Koullette: Just answer the question. I need you [to] be certain that these are bats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm… I want to like this more than I do. The whole worms eye view with our very own Rosencrantz and the corpse of Guildenstern is an interesting change of pace… but it leaves out far too much for my tastes. For me, the aforementioned play works because we already have Hamlet to work off of, where here it's just… I dunno. Just my personal opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All that stuff does come through in the writing, so good job on that. I guess I'm just still stuck on the fact that despite knowing how important this "test" is, nobody in the Foundation replaces or better supports Raven. It's probably a fairly realistic situation but it still doesn't feel satisfying to me. Probably just personal taste

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great read with excellent pace and captivating dialogue. Also, the length of this entry is perfect. Many of these 5K entries are so long that they are starting to feel like a chore.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite examples of how to use redactions to enhance instead of subtract from a story ever. Easy +1
And before the end of the contest too! I'm glad you've all enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this one hurt. Like swallowing a stone. This to me, more than anything else I've read in a long time, reminded me just how terrible and cruel the foundation is. More than blood. More than torture. Just… tunnels, and terror, and lies, and darkness.

Magnificent. I think this would be a magnificent 5000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
jesus christ, this is bleak. I'm sitting with a friend and they just gave me some weird looks because I essentially yelled "hey what the fuck?" at my phone.

I like that this is a human story … the redactions broadly tell the story of the anomaly (and what a story—loved the gradual escalation), but the real, non redacted meat is the story of Charles and Raven. poor fucking Charles. :'( poor Thomas, too, but we don't really get the opportunity to get attached to him. and I can understand why Charles did what he did. desperation is a terrible thing. it makes us act in ways we'd never think to otherwise. he's gonna die all alone down there and he knows it and all he has left is the light and heat. he can't handle the thought of dying of dehydration/ starvation in the dark. I can't even say I blame him, even knowing there could be a lot at stake.

this has basically all been said already so this comment is fairly pointless… but a bummed out +1 from me. lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, but it doesn't feel like horror to me. I think this stems both from the pacing and the imagery. For one, the story progresses too quickly to capture and maintain a sense of dread. I don't believe this to be detrimental, because the strength of the interviews, and the source of tension, is primarily the character interaction rather than the atmosphere (though the latter still plays a lesser role). As for the imagery, dark tunnels and bats are classics in this regard, but they're not really the focus here, so it ultimately doesn't move beyond eeriness into horror at any point for me. Again though, this isn't a weakness, just an observation given that many commenters experienced this as a horror story while I didn't.

That all being said, the greatest strengths are the character interaction and redaction. The former is executed to a very high level, especially where the mutual deception is concerned, while the latter is a clever use of obfuscation that I'd love to see more of in future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I totally understand why this may no gel for you, I do have some internal reasoning about why Raven acts the way she does. Raven is not one of the people at Site-19 who is trained explicitly to interact with D-Class. The whole problem of "how do we get this person to do what we want" was supposed to be Thomas' role. But, since Thomas gets taken out of the picture pretty quickly, that task falls to Raven.

spoilers part of the justification
– hide block

So, while there's a lot of things Raven definitely could have done much, much better, when she is confronting Charles at the end, both of them are out of their comfort zones. They're yelling, they're panicking, and in that kind of situation its a lot harder to come up with lies to tell if you're not trained for it.

Raven lies to Charles throughout the article but she's never really forced to back it up until that moment, and in that moment she fumbles it.

I guess there's an argument that she should've been replaced but since everything else had been going smoothly with Charles up to that point, and also because of all the craziness and bureaucracy that goes on at the Foundation, no one probably thought to make such a replacement.

TLDR; You're right. Raven is really bad at convincing Charles to do things, but that's because that's not her job. She kind of gets away with bullshitting it until Charles calls her out, and that's when it falls apart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Near as I can tell...
begone guesses

The d-class starts doing the testing things, which breaks the connection between site 19 and this power generator complex. The connection starts generating weirdness that pushes upwards towards the main site. The person on the radio wants the d-class to finish the testing stuff because he (hopes/knows) that doing so will fix the problem, but the d-class refuses and eventually the weirdness forces the site to be abandoned.

Which bothers me because
begone thots

The radio dude is really bad at convincing the d-class to finish the stuff! "Why do you want me to push this button so badly?" "Sorry, can't say, classified." If he couldn't explain the real reason, why not lie? Why not say "We lied to you, this has happened before. Our 'testing' is actually maintenance, finish the tests and push emergency and everything will be fixed and we'll be able to come get you"?

tldr I'm not gonna vote on this. Interesting (and ambitious) idea, but the story my brain filled into the missing bits doesn't quite fit together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't really disagree with everyone who says the parts are all fantastic, because they are.

But from my perspective, they don't gel into anything. This guy's just gonna sit there and starve to death because no one's coming to get him/he's not gettable because anomalies, the end? I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed the main story! As for your comment about the anomaly, I was hoping that the

Inspection of the Site-19 blueprints indicated that these tunnels were never originally meant to be part of the site's construction.

would be enough to lampshade it until the logs. Although I understand if that's not quite enough to make it obviously anomalous from just the description alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm Commander Shephard and this is my favorite entry in the contest.

Although factors conspired to stop me from reading this for a few days, it was a treat to finally get to. This article does so much lifting via what isn't said that all propel Charles toward the end. This is a piece I am going to enjoy re-reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was incredible. I love how the situation down there is relatively stable, but up top you can see it getting progressively worse and worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is how you use redactions. authors should take note of this masterful execution of not just obfuscation of information, but implementation of character dynamics and dialogue. charles' emotional journey was a wonderful one to follow and i could basically feel the dread setting in as the logs went on. well done, easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just

God, Kirby you're a fucking incredible author and reading stuff like this makes me wonder why it's not rated higher. The evocation of mystery to the extent of horror rather than intrigue really pulls you in from the get go, and even though I thought it would be a long read, each collapsible sucked me in more and more and more.

Resounding +1. Just really fucking good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Genius use of redactions! I think you nailed the mystery theme perfectly. I'm left with a lot of curiosity and urge to know more, but the really good "my imagination fills in the blanks… Spooky!" kind, not the annoying kind. I liked the containment procedures as well lol. It's actually kind of scary considering this is a safe skip, not neutralized despite mysteriously destroying Site-19. Whatever caused all that chaos is still in there…

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The information only being given via the title of the censored document is a great idea but Aaaaaaaah i want to know more.

Upvote.

Man this contest's theme is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy to hear my dialogue has its own brand now :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really phenomenal. Many of the other comments captured critiques of the article that I wanted to mention, but I just had to comment.

The use of a seemingly, even deceptively simple anomaly, the lack of information the reader has about it, and the various parallel storylines with the D-class, the research head, the various Site-19 operations was fantastic.

Strong +1. Definitely a great mystery skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this during the drafting phase, and I love it now.

Originally I was torn on the fact that so much of the "supplemental material" was redacted, but upon seeing the titles of those documents, and gaining a handle on what was happening, I have to say you really made it work, CK. You have really made me want to know more.

As others have said, the atmosphere in this piece is quite well built throughout the duration of the read.

A solid +1. An excellent contest entry, and an excellent SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful. I would like to say more, since that's a 5k con entry, but that perfectly summarizes my feelings. Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like the skip! We get just enough pieces of the puzzle to form an idea but the full picture is left to our own imagination. The slowly escalating response/attempts by the foundation to reach the two (and them eventually giving up and evacuating the whole site), plus the setting of the story in a cave/tunnel helps create a lot of tension. Definitely helps that we're not even sure who's quite telling the truth the whole time.

Fits the whole mystery theme of the contest very well and is a good read, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this a lot. As others have said, the clever dual story-telling using "figure and ground", the duplicitous characters, the sense of mystery and the lovely reversal of the expected meaning of the title (ie "who is actually powerless here?") were all very neat.

The only thing that threw me in the early going is that it's not immediately clear that a tunnel system of this type is anomalous. Even a lampshade that this isn't on site plans, or spontaneously appeared / was discovered, might help that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… This made me cry. It hit really hard, and I don't know entirely why.

I had more lined up to talk about specific details, but none of that seems important right now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: im gonna make u history

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. This. I like this. I like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 4kon was history, 3kon was horror

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Koullette: Ok. Well, more food is a good thing I guess. It'll probably be easier to feed Thomas the berries than the rations.

Yeah I wouldn't be suspicious of those either.

Supplemental Documentation
Compromise of Site-19 Sublevels 36-38

I knew paying attention to these would pique my interest at some point.

Alright, and after reading this, it's an upvote. I don't know if I have too much to say about it either. I liked that as it went on, we were getting less and less information, feeling more in the dark. I loved the supplemental documentation gimmick — it made sense in-universe and really upped the mystery (ooo there's that 5K theme wooah whaaat). It gave a lot more credence to the fact that they were having trouble opening a door. Because either they weren't, or there is more to that door than is in this document. Also, you effectively had [DATA EXPUNGED], but it was access levels required, and never in the middle of paragraphs, and you still got the titles of the documents (it immediately caught my eye that the Ethics Committee was involved), and just, yeah, well done. Good article. +1

Seriously though what was with that berry exchange?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has the spirit of old-school creep, redactions and D-Class explorations included

I was definitely aiming for recreating the old-school redaction feel, but I totally forgot about the D-Class exploration trope! :P

Seriously, thanks for the kind words and I'm glad you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit Kirby 3Kon already happened, you don't need to do a second go-round at the horror theme.

This has the spirit of old-school creep, redactions and D-Class explorations included, with the best of the new-school writing sensibilities. I don't think you need anyone else to tell you how good you are at dialogue, but in case you do I'm here. Just the kind of thing I want to read on a cold rainy morning with nobody else in the house.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this piece for it's atmosphere and Kirby's brand of snippy, witty dialogue and ability to capture character voices. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding gee's crit with not much more to add.
Loved the dialogue, the secondary thread told through the classified logs, and how the story falls together at the end. Easily one of the strongest contest contenders. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the creeping despair that you see manifesting in Charles as the days roll by

\o/! I was kind of hoping for a very isolated feeling. Also why I love the title of this article too :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Something that had kind of gone out of style that I wanted to bring back was redaction. I like that the site makes less use of redaction overall, because bad redaction can hurt an article, but I realized that the best way to make the unseen events of the story feel big, is through redaction. I'm happy it didn't backfire!

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, I love this one.

[+ Opinion]
– hide block

Every part of this felt like it was contributing to the piece as a whole. I started off unsure about these classified documents but as it progressed I was more and more interested. They're a unique method of telling a secondary story and one I feel really paid off.

The fact that both characters hid information from each other was pretty great, and unexpected for me. I'm slightly sick of articles where the only moral grey areas come from the Foundation, and the reveal that the D-Class had been taking all the food subverted this wonderfully. Other aspects of characterisation were also exemplary - this was just one part that stuck out as especially good.

Overall, this ties together to form a great and cohesive piece. I'll definitely second sterbai's comment above; the angle you've gone for with the two interlinked stories is unique and remarkably fitting for this contest's theme. Well done.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the creeping despair that you see manifesting in Charles as the days roll by. Excellent atmosphere Kirby, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely one of my favourites.

I love the atmosphere and the dialogue is excellent. I love the way that the mystery is given here - we only get the perspective of one man, while bigger events play out that we only get a snippet of. Very well done.

+1 obviously

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope you all enjoy my entry to 5Kcon! It was originally based off of a twist on the "we die in the dark so others may live in the light mantra", and some of that is still there but its moved away from that. Hopefully it still is powerful.

I'd like to thank ihp, Tufto, oboegeek99, NamelessMediocre, DrBleep, Croquembouche, Dyslexion, Conwell, Sterbai, ARD, and T Rutherford for looking this over and giving me feedback. Without you guys this probably wouldn't have evolved into the piece I have here.

And thank you, good reader, for giving my piece the time of day.

Image credit: https://www.flickr.com/photos/93425126@N00/313291902

Happy 5Kcon!

Slot Preferences:

5002
5003
5004
5005
5010
